coredata.dc:title: The effect of pyrrolic nitrogen on corrosion inhibition performance of N-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Siyan
Wang, Jing
Wang, Zhiqiang
Zhang, Litao
Xu, Hongyan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using a two-step hydrothermal method. Initially, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from citric acid, followed by nitrogen doping with ammonia solution. By varying the heating temperature in the second step, three types of NCDs were synthesized containing amino-N, pyrrolic-N, or quaternary-N. The corrosion inhibition properties of these NCDs on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl were investigated. The NCDs with the highest pyrrolic-N content (4.10 at.%) demonstrated superior corrosion inhibition, with an efficiency of 96.63 % at 200 mg/L, underscoring the vital role of pyrrolic-N. Quantum chemical calculations elucidated the underlying mechanism: NCDs adsorbed parallelly onto the carbon steel surface by donating their π-electrons to Fe atoms, and forming a protective film that segregated the steel from the acid solution. The delocalization of p-orbital electrons from pyrrolic-N into π-conjugation altered the energies of the π-electrons within the NCDs, enhancing their reactivity and electron-donation ability, thereby augmenting their adsorption capability.

head.ce:title: The effect of pyrrolic nitrogen on corrosion inhibition performance of N-doped carbon dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were synthesized using a two-step hydrothermal method. Initially, carbon dots (CDs) were prepared from citric acid, followed by nitrogen doping with ammonia solution. By varying the heating temperature in the second step, three types of NCDs were synthesized containing amino-N, pyrrolic-N, or quaternary-N. The corrosion inhibition properties of these NCDs on Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl were investigated. The NCDs with the highest pyrrolic-N content (4.10 at.%) demonstrated superior corrosion inhibition, with an efficiency of 96.63 % at 200 mg/L, underscoring the vital role of pyrrolic-N. Quantum chemical calculations elucidated the underlying mechanism: NCDs adsorbed parallelly onto the carbon steel surface by donating their π-electrons to Fe atoms, and forming a protective film that segregated the steel from the acid solution. The delocalization of p-orbital electrons from pyrrolic-N into π-conjugation altered the energies of the π-electrons within the NCDs, enhancing their reactivity and electron-donation ability, thereby augmenting their adsorption capability.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to its exceptional mechanical properties and cost-effectiveness, carbon steel has emerged as one of the most extensively utilized steels within various industrial applications. However, its relatively low corrosion resistance represents a critical issue, potentially culminating in severe operational failures [1–3]. This challenge necessitates the implementation of effective mitigation strategies. Corrosion inhibitors offer a practical solution by significantly curtailing the corrosion rate of carbon steel, and they carry the benefits of cost-effectiveness and simplicity in application. However, a noteworthy concern stems from the inherent toxicity of many organic corrosion inhibitors commonly employed in industrial contexts, which can lead to health and environmental implications [4,5]. In the current era of increasing environmental consciousness and health awareness, the emphasis on developing efficient, environmentally benign corrosion inhibitors has intensified.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of zero-dimensional materials, have been widely used in many fields. Their primary applications include biological imaging [6,7], ion sensing [8,9], energy storage [10,11], and photocatalytic processes [12,13]. Recently, CDs' potential as environmentally friendly corrosion inhibitors has garnered significant attention from the research community. This is due to their inherent benefits, including high water solubility, impressive adsorptive capacity, good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and cost-effectiveness [14]. CDs have exhibited substantial efficacy in preventing corrosion of various metals across a multitude of environments. For instance, in the study conducted by Zhang et al. [15], nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) synthesized from citric acid and L-serine demonstrated a significant ability to inhibit corrosion of copper, reaching an inhibition efficiency as high as 98.5 % in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. In a similar vein, NCDs prepared by Zhu et al. [16] effectively inhibited corrosion of Q235 steel in a 1 M HCl solution, achieving a corrosion inhibition efficiency of 95.4 % at an NCDs concentration of 150 mg/L. Furthermore, Cen [17] and his colleagues pioneered the application of N, S co-doped CDs as corrosion inhibitors for Al alloy in a 0.1 M HCl solution, resulting in an inhibition efficiency of 85 % at a concentration of 5 mg/L. Additionally, the study of Ye and his colleagues [18] illustrated that CDs also exhibited potent inhibition effects on the corrosion of carbon steel in 3.5 wt.% NaCl solutions.

Two primarily methods have been employed to enhance the corrosion inhibiting performance of carbon dots (CDs): one is modifying CDs with ionic liquids [19], and the other is doping CDs with heteroatoms such as nitrogen (N) or sulfur (S) atoms [17,20–23]. Among these methods, heteroatom doping has gained the favor of a large number of researchers for its simple operation, easy control of raw material costs, and the reproducibility of the properties of the synthesized products. The role of heteroatoms, including N, O, phosphorus (P), and sulfur (S), in organic corrosion inhibitors, is widely acknowledged [24,25]. These atoms can donate their lone pair electrons to the metal surface, facilitating the formation of coordination bonds between the metal atoms and the corrosion inhibitor molecules. This process enhances the adsorption of the corrosion inhibitor onto the metal surface, leading to the subsequent formation of a protective film. Similarly, π-electrons also have this effect [26,27]. These principles can be applied to elucidate the inhibition mechanism of CDs inhibitors that contain both heteroatoms and π-electrons [20,28].

Nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have been recognized for their superior corrosion inhibition performance. They have four distinct forms of nitrogen atoms: amino-N, pyrrolic-N, quaternary-N, and pyridinic-N [29,30]. These four forms of nitrogen atoms differ in their electron-donating capabilities due to variations in their atomic surroundings. Additionally, these nitrogen atoms potentially influence the energy of π-electrons within the planar conjugate structure of NCDs [31]. A comprehensive exploration of the relationship between the form of nitrogen atoms and the corrosion inhibition performance of NCDs, along with the corresponding mechanisms, is of great significance to improve the corrosion inhibition property of NCDs.

In order to investigate the effect of nitrogen form on the corrosion inhibition performance of NCDs, three types of NCDs were synthesized by a two-step hydrothermal synthesis method in this article. The content of various nitrogen forms within these NCDs was modulated by altering the heating temperature to different values during the second step. Their structures and components were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The effect of N form on the corrosion inhibition performance of the NCDs was evaluated by investigating their inhibition effects on the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in a 1 M HCl solution. This evaluation involved methods such as weight loss measurements, potentiodynamic polarization, and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). Furthermore, the mechanism by which N forms influence the corrosion inhibitory properties of NCDs was elucidated using density functional theory (DFT).

Citric acid monohydrate (≥99.5 wt.%), ammonia solution (NH3 25–28 wt.%) and hydrochloric acid (HCl 36–38 wt.%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). Carbon steel Q235 was provided by local suppliers. Deionized water (18 MΩ cm) was used throughout all experiments.

The NCDs were synthesized via a two-step hydrothermal synthesis method, as depicted in Fig. 1 . Firstly, carbon dots (CDs) were generated using the following procedure: 0.42 g of citric acid monohydrate was dissolved in 10 mL of deionized water, creating a colorless and transparent solution. This solution was then transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 5 h. At the end of this process, the solution assumed a light-yellow color, indicating the formation of large-sized polymer nanoparticles and carbon dots (CDs). Subsequently, the obtained CDs were doped with nitrogen using a hydrothermal synthesis method. 535 μL of ammonia solution was added to the 10 mL of yellow CDs solution, and this mixture was heated at different temperatures in a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave for 5 h. Upon completion of the reaction, NCDs were synthesised within the solution. The solution retained its light-yellow color under natural light but emitted blue light when excited with a 365 nm UV beam. The NCDs synthesized at 100 °C, 160 °C, and 220 °C during the second step were designated as NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, respectively. Then, the impurities were removed by dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off ∼1000 Da) for 3 days, with the deionized water replaced every 3 h. The purified products were then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h to yield a powder of NCDs.

The morphological properties of the NCDs were characterized utilizing a FEI Tecnai F30 field emission TEM (Thermo Fisher Inc., USA, operated at 300 kV). The Thermo Fisher 250xi X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrometer was employed to assess the chemical composition and bonding states of the NCDs, operating under a vacuum pressure of less than 5 × 10−10 Bar. The spectrometer utilized a monochromatic Al-Kα radiation source with a photon energy of 1486.6 eV. Wide scans were conducted using a fixed analyzer transmission (FAT) energy of 280 eV, with incremental steps of 1 eV. For the detailed chemical state analyses, narrow scans were performed within the binding energy range, using a FAT energy of 112 eV with incremental steps of 0.1 eV. For calibration purposes, the binding energies were referenced to the C 1s peak at 284.8 eV. The narrow scan spectra from the XPS were deconvoluted using XPSPEAK41 Software, employing a nonlinear Shirley-type background subtraction and a Gaussian (20 %)-Lorentzian (80 %) mixed function. To accurately determine the atomic percentage of each chemical state of elements C, N, and O, the area under each corresponding peak was integrated. In other words, the atomic percentage was calculated by dividing the area of the peak of a specific chemical state by the total area under all peaks.

The corrosion rate of steel was carried out using weight loss measurement according to the National Association of Corrosion Engineers Standard (NACE TM0169/G31) [32]. The specimens for the weight loss measurements were Q235 carbon steel sheets with dimensions of 10 mm × 20 mm × 1 mm. Prior to the measurements, all specimens were polished with 320, 800, 1000, and 2000 mesh sandpapers. Subsequently, they were cleaned with ethanol and air-dried. The immersion solution was the 1 M HCl solutions with varying concentrations of NCDs: 0 mg/L, 50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, 150 mg/L, and 200 mg/L. The dimensions and weights of the specimens were measured using a vernier caliper and an electronic balance, respectively. The corrosion rates (v, mg/m2 h), the inhibition efficiencies (ηW ) and the surface coverage area (θW ) were calculated based on the results of weight loss measurement using the following equations:

For Eq. (1), W0 and W represent the weight of Q235 carbon steel specimens before and after immersion, respectively; s denotes the surface area of the specimens; and t refers to the immersion time. The v0 and v in Eq. (2) correspond to the corrosion rates of the specimens in the 1 M HCl solution without and with NCDs, respectively.

All potentiodynamic polarization and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy EIS measurements were conducted using a three-electrode system on a CHI 660E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua Inc., China). The Q235 carbon steel was employed as the working electrode, which was mounted in epoxy resin with an exposed area of 10 × 10 mm. The counter electrode was a piece of Pt foil (10 mm × 10 mm × 1 mm), and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) served as the reference electrode. Prior to each electrochemical measurement, the working electrode surface was mechanically polished using 320, 800, 1000, and 2000 mesh sandpapers. It was subsequently cleaned with ethanol and air-dried. The test solutions were the 1 M HCl solutions with varying concentrations of NCDs: 0 mg/L, 50 mg/L, 100 mg/L, 150 mg/L, and 200 mg/L.

Potentiodynamic polarization and EIS measurements were performed after a 24-h immersion period of the working electrode in the aforementioned solutions. The potential range for the potentiodynamic polarization measurement was ±300 mV relative to the open circuit potential (OCP) of the Q235 carbon steel, utilizing a scan rate of 1 mV/s. EIS measurements were conducted over a frequency range spanning from 105 Hz to 10−2 Hz at the OCP, with an amplitude of 5 mV.

The surface morphology of the Q235 carbon steel sheets was characterized using an EVO18 scanning electron microscope (SEM, Carl Zeiss Inc., Germany) operating at a voltage of 20 kV. This characterization was carried out after a 24-h immersion in the 1 M HCl solutions with and without various types of NCDs (200 mg/L).

For the computational analysis, Gaussian 16 software was utilized to calculate the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), and other quantum chemical parameters of the NCDs. The NCDs models were optimized at the density functional theory (DFT) level using the B3LYP method in conjunction with a 6–311G ++ (d, p) basis set. The solvent environment was set as water. For each model of NCD which contains amino-N or pyrrolic-N atoms, a neutral charge was assigned. Conversely, for the models of the NCD which contains quaternary-N atoms, each N atom in the model attributed a single positive charge.

Fig. 2 presents the morphological characteristics of the undoped CDs and the three types of nitrogen-doped CDs (NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3). The TEM image of the CDs (Fig. 2(a)), reveals the presence of nanoparticles with an average diameter of 2.85 nm, in conjunction with certain polymer-like structures. However, these polymer-like structures are absent in the TEM images of NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, as illustrated in Fig. 2(c), (e) and (g), respectively. This observation may be related to the four-step formation process of CDs and NCDs: dehydration, polymerization, aromatization, and carbonization [33–35]. The polymer-like structures (as shown in Fig. 2(a)) were formed following the dehydration process, and the nanoparticles nucleated on these structures, subsequently forming individual nanoparticles through the aromatization and carbonization processes [33–35]. Consequently, extending the synthesis time [35] or increasing the synthesis temperature [33,34] might facilitate the transformation of polymer-like structures into NCD particles. The average diameters of NCDs-1, NCDs-2 and NCDs-3 were 2.84 nm, 2.46 nm, and 2.41 nm, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2(c), (e) and (g). The average diameters of the three types of NCDs exhibited minor variations. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of the CDs and the NCDs-1, NCDs-2 and NCDs-3 are displayed in Fig. 2(b), (d), (f) and (h), respectively. The observed lattice fringes, which have a spacing of 0.21 nm, correspond to the (1 0 0) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [36].

The XPS wide scan spectra of NCDs-1, NCDs-2 and NCDs-3 are shown in Fig. 3 (a), along with the relative atomic contents of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) for these three types of NCDs. Specifically, NCDs-1 had 50.33 at.% C, 4.70 at.% N, and 22.11 at.% O; NCDs-2 contained 58.83 at.% C, 8.57 at.% N, and 35.60 at.% O; while NCDs-3 consisted of 63.19 at.% C, 7.23 at.% N, and 29.58 at.% O. It is noteworthy that NCDs-1 exhibited the lowest C content and the highest O content among the three types of NCDs, while NCDs-3 demonstrated the highest C content and the lowest O content. Considering the synthesis conditions, NCDs-1 were synthesized at the lowest temperature during the nitrogen doping step, compared to NCDs-2 and NCDs-3. Conversely, NCDs-3 were synthesized at the highest temperature. A higher synthesis temperature may facilitate an increased degree of dehydration and carbonization, leading to a decrease in O content and a concurrent increase in C content [37]. Fig. 3(b) to (d) exhibit the N 1s, C 1s and O 1s narrow scan spectra of three types of NCDs, respectively. Within the N 1s narrow scan spectra (Fig. 3(b)), the observed peaks at 399.4 ± 0.1 eV, 400.2 ± 0.1 eV, and 400.9 ± 0.1 eV were associated with amino-N, pyrrolic-N, and quaternary-N, respectively [16,29,31,38–40]. In Fig. 3(b), the pyrrolic-N peak of NCDs-3 exhibited a negative shift of 0.2 eV relative to those of NCDs-1 and NCDs-2. This could potentially be ascribed to an increase of defects in NCDs-3 [41]. The peaks of C 1s narrow scan spectra at 284.7 ± 0.1 eV, 285.4 ± 0.1 eV, 287.3 ± 0.3 eV and 288.5 ± 0.2 eV were attributed to CC/CC, CO/CN, CN and CO, respectively [31,40]. The peaks of O 1s narrow scan spectra in Fig. 3(d) at 531.5 ± 0.2 eV and 532.8 eV corresponded to CO and CO, respectively [16].

Table 1 details the content of various nitrogen and oxygen forms, including quaternary-N, pyrrolic-N, amino-N, O of CO bonds (O of CO), and O of CO bonds (O of CO), along with their total amounts in the three types of NCDs. Among the three types of NCDs, NCDs-2 had the highest total N content. Particularly noteworthy is the fact that NCDs-3 possessed the highest pyrrolic-N content at 4.10 at.%, while the NCDs-1 exhibited the lowest pyrrolic-N content at 2.30 at.%. In terms of O content, NCDs-1 had the highest total O content, as well as the highest content of O of CO bond and O of CO bond. Conversely, NCDs-3 displayed the lowest total O content, along with the lowest content in both O of CO bond and O of CO bond.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Weight loss measurements and adsorption isotherm study
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 (a) and (b) respectively demonstrate the corrosion rates and inhibition efficiencies (as well as the surface coverage area) of Q235 carbon steel after immersion for varying durations (2 h, 6 h, 12 h, 24 h, and 48 h) in the 1 M HCl solutions, each contains 0 mg/L and 200 mg/L of NCDs. Analysis of these figures reveals a substantial inhibitory effect of all three types of NCDs on the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in the 1 M HCl solution. The inhibition efficiencies for these three NCDs show a marked increase during the first 6 h, followed by a gradual ascent between 6 to 24 h, and eventual stabilization after 24 h. This observed trend can be attributed to the adsorption process of NCDs on the Q235 carbon steel surface, leading to the formation of an NCDs film. Within the initial 24-h immersion period, the surface coverage of the NCDs film progressively increased with the extension of immersion time. Post the 24-h mark, the surface coverage of the NCDs film exhibited minimal change. This stabilization can be ascribed to the attainment of dynamic equilibrium between the adsorption and desorption processes of the NCDs inhibitors on the Q235 carbon steel surface, thereby maintaining a consistent corrosion efficiency [42], resulting in a consistent corrosion efficiency. After a 24-h immersion, the recorded inhibition efficiencies for NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3 were 88.40 ± 0.40 %, 92.56 ± 0.37 %, and 96.63 ± 0.07 %, respectively. Notably, throughout the entire immersion process, the inhibition efficiencies of the three types of NCDs were ranked as follows: NCDs-3 > NCDs-2 > NCDs-1.

The stability of NCDs-3 was also evaluated. As depicted in Fig. S1(a), the corrosion inhibition efficiency of NCDs-3 remained as high as 96.74 %, even after six days of immersion. Furthermore, following a six-day immersion experiment, the testing solution, containing 200 mg/L NCDs and 1 M HCl, retained its clarity and transparency, without any precipitate, as illustrated in Fig. S1(b). This suggests that NCDs-3 exhibits excellent dispersibility in 1 M HCl solutions for a duration of at least six days.

The corrosion rates of Q235 carbon steel after 24-h immersion in a 1 M HCl solution with various concentrations of NCDs are depicted in Fig. 4(c). Correspondingly, the associated inhibition efficiencies, along with the surface coverage area of the three types of NCDs, are presented in Fig. 4(d). From these figures, it can be discerned that the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel decreased significantly with the increasing concentration of NCDs, leading to enhanced inhibition efficiencies. This trend is consistent with the understanding that a higher concentration of corrosion inhibitors generally promotes greater surface coverage [43]. Moreover, Fig. 4(d) reveals that among the three types of NCDs inhibitors, NCDs-3 demonstrated the highest corrosion inhibition efficiency, while NCDs-1 exhibited the lowest. This observation aligns with the results presented in Fig. 4(b). Compared with the CDs inhibitors which have been already reported, the corrosion inhibition efficiency, and the values of Kads and
Δ

a d s
0
of the NCDs-3 are comparable, as shown in Table S1.

The results of weight loss measurements showed that the NCDs-3 had the best corrosion inhibition performance among the three types of NCDs inhibitors, followed by NCDs-2, while NCDs-1 exhibits the lowest corrosion inhibitory efficiency. The nano-size and the N content were discussed as two factors that may affect the inhibition properties of NCDs, in the works of Cen [17] and Saraswat [44], respectively. In Cen's work [17], the N, S co-doped CDs demonstrated lower corrosion inhibition efficiency compared to conventional inhibitors due to their larger size (ranging from 10 to 20 nm). While the typical sizes of the ordinary molecular corrosion inhibitors range from 0.1 to 5 nm. However, the TEM results (Fig. 2) revealed that the three forms of NCDs prepared in this study had similar particle sizes with average diameters of 2.84 nm, 2.46 nm, and 2.41 nm, respectively. Therefore, the size of NCDs cannot be identified as the primary factor affecting their corrosion inhibition properties in this study. Regarding the N content, Saraswat [44] and his colleagues identified that NCDs with a higher N content (13.4 at.%) showed an improved inhibition efficiency compared to those with a lower N content (4.2 at.%). However, in the present study, NCDs-3, with an N content of 7.23 at.%, demonstrated superior inhibition properties over NCDs-2, containing 8.56 at.% N, as validated by XPS results (Fig. 3 and Table 1). This observation led to the conclusion that the total N content alone cannot be identified as the primary determinant for corrosion inhibition properties in this work. What emerges as a critical factor should be the form of N atom in NCDs. Notably, the pyrrolic-N content across the three types of NCDs followed the order: NCDs-3 > NCDs-2 > NCDs-1, which was the same as the observed order of their corrosion efficiencies. This correlation strongly indicates that the pyrrolic-N content significantly influenced the inhibition properties of NCDs.

To discern the adsorption mechanism of the NCDs, an array of adsorption isotherm models, including Langmuir, Temkin, El-Awady, Flory-Huggins, and Freundlich [45], were deployed to fit the corresponding outcomes deduced from the weight loss measurements. According to the linear regression coefficients (R 2) engendered from the diverse adsorption isotherm models (Refer to Fig. S2, Table S1, and Fig. 5 ), the R 2 values of the Langmuir isotherm were the closest to 1. This indicates that the Langmuir isotherm offers a more precise representation of the interaction between the NCDs and the steel surface. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm was calculated from Eq. These free energy values fall within the range of −20 kJ/mol to −40 kJ/mol[48–50], implying that the interactions between NCDs and the Q235 carbon steel surface encompassed both chemisorption and physisorption. ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of immersion temperature and the thermodynamic parameter calculations
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the effect of immersion temperature on the corrosion inhibition performance of the three types of NCDs inhibitors, weight loss measurements were carried out. These tests determined the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solutions, each containing 200 mg/L NCDs-1, NCDs-2 and NCDs-3, at temperatures of 35 °C, 45 °C, and 55 °C respectively. Following this, the corresponding corrosion inhibition efficiencies were computed. The results are displayed in Fig. 6 , where Fig. 6(a) presents the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel, and Fig. 6(b) shows the corresponding corrosion inhibition efficiencies of the NCDs. From these graphs, it is evident that as the immersion temperature increased, the corrosion rates of Q235 carbon steel immersed in three different HCl solutions, each containing NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, also accelerated (Fig. 6(a)). Correspondingly, the corrosion inhibition efficiency of the three NCDs decreased with the rise of temperature. Specifically, the efficiency of NCDs-3 reached 96.63 % at 25 °C and maintained a rate above 90 % (90.89 %) at 45 °C, but when the immersion temperature rose to 55 °C, its corrosion inhibition efficiency rapidly declined to 76.91 %. Additionally, at any temperature, the corrosion inhibition efficiencies of NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3 follow the order: NCDs-1 < NCDs-2 < NCDs-3.

According to the obtained corrosion rate results, the activation and thermodynamic parameters were calculated. The apparent activation energy (Ea ) of metal dissolution was calculated by applying the Arrhenius equation [51,52]:
(6)

where vh signifies the corrosion rate of the Q235 carbon steel, denoted in mm/year, A represents the rate constant, R is the universal gas constant (valued at 8.314 J/(mol K)), and T is the absolute temperature. Fig. 7 (a) illustrates the plots of lnvh vs. 1000/T, which were employed to evaluate Ea , as determined by the slope of the plot at each temperature.

The activation enthalpy (ΔHa ) and activation entropy (ΔSa ) values were ascertained via the transition-state equation [51,52]:

In this equation, N stands for Avogadro's number (valued at 6.022 × 1023), while h is Plank's constant (valued at 6.626 × 10−34 J s). ΔHa and ΔSa were respectively derived from the slope and intercept of the ln(vh ⁄T) vs. 1/T plot, as depicted in Fig.7(b).

Table 2 lists the values of activation parameters at various temperatures. The Ea of the corrosion process of Q235 carbon steel sheets which were immersed in the blank HCl solution and the HCl solutions individually containing NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, exhibited a distinct sequence: NCDs-3 > NCDs-2 > NCDs-1 > HCl. Elevated value of Ea generally lead to a decelerated corrosion rate. This suggests that the adsorption of NCDs on Q235 carbon steel surface slowed down its corrosion process. The positive value of ∆Ha indicated the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel was an endothermic process. The ΔS a values measured in the HCl solution with NCDs-3 was more positive than that in the HCl solution with NCDs-1 or NCDs-2, which indicated that the adsorption of NCDs-3 may removal of a greater number of adsorbed water molecules from the Q235 carbon steel surface than NCDs-1 and NCDs-2 [52].

Figure S3 illustrates the OCP values of Q235 carbon steel recorded after its immersion in a blank 1 M HCl solution and the HCl solutions individually containing 200 mg/L NCDs-1, NCDs-2 and NCDs-3 over different periods of time. The OCP of Q235 in 1 M HCl was noted to be −0.422 V vs. SCE, which gradually declined to −0.443 V vs. SCE in following 46 h. However, upon the initial immersion of Q235 carbon steel for 2 h in the HCl solutions individually containing NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, its OCP further descended to −0.479, −0.482, and −0.486 V vs. SCE, respectively. Subsequently, the OCP of Q235 steel immersed in the HCl solution with NCDs exhibited a tendency to oscillate towards the negative side. After an immersion period of 48 h, Q235 carbon steel in a HCl solution containing 200 mg/L of NCDs-3 demonstrated the lowest OCP, recording a value of -0.529 V vs. SCE. Meanwhile, Q235 carbon steel immersed in HCl solutions each containing NCDs-1 and NCDs-2 recorded OCP values of −0.499 and −0.523 V vs. SCE, respectively. This negative shift indicated that the inhibition effect of NCDs on the hydrogen ion reduction reaction (cathodic reaction) surpassed that on the Fe dissolution reaction (anodic reaction). In addition, a greater the negative OCP shift corresponds to a stronger the cathodic polarization of corrosion reaction.

The potentiodynamic polarization curves for Q235 carbon steel, measured in 1 M HCl solution with varying concentrations of NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3 inhibitors after a 24-h immersion period, are depicted in Fig. 8 (a)–(c), respectively. Table 3 summarizes the key parameters essential parameters derived from the polarization curves, including corrosion potential (Ecorr ), corrosion current density (icorr ), anodic Tafel slope (ba ) and cathodic Tafel slope (bc ). The notable decrease in icorr for Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution upon the introduction of the three types of NCDs illustrates the inhibitive capability of these NCDs against the corrosion behavior of Q235 carbon steel. Furthermore, the icorr value demonstrated a consistent reduction with increasing NCDs concentration, a trend that aligns with the weight loss measurement results shown in Fig. 4(c). The ba and −bc values of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution with varying NCDs concentrations were higher than those of Q235 carbon steel in the blank HCl solution. The increment in ba and bc values, upon NCDs application, signified the inhibition of the dissolution reaction of Fe and the reduction reaction of hydrogen ions, attributed to the adsorbed NCDs. In Table 3, the ηi and θi represent the inhibition efficiencies and surface coverage of three types of NCDs, respectively. These values were calculated from the results of potentiodynamic polarization measurements. The inhibition efficiencies (ηi ) and surface coverage (θi ) can be computed using the following equations:

The i
corr0 and icorr in Eqs. (8) and (9) correspond to the corrosion current density of the specimens in the 1 M HCl solution without and with NCDs, respectively. It can be discerned that the inhibition effect of NCDs escalated with rising concentration, and the order of corrosion inhibition properties for these three types of NCDs was: NCDs-3 > NCDs-2 > NCDs-1.

Fig. 9 (a)–(c) depict the Nyquist plots for Q235 carbon steel following a 24-h immersion in 1 M HCl solution with varying concentrations of NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, respectively. In Fig. 9 the dots represent the empirically measured values, and the corresponding fitted values are illustrated by solid lines. The measurements taken in blank HCl solution were modeled using the LRs(QdlRct) fitting, while those in HCl solution containing NCDs inhibitors were accommodated by the LRs(Qf(Rf(QdlRct))) model [18,53], as shown in Fig. 10 . The inductance L accounts for the electromagnetic induction of the testing device; the equivalent resistance R s corresponds to the ohmic resistance of the electrolyte; the constant phase element Qdl symbolizes the double-layer capacitance; R ct signifies the resistance governing the kinetics of the Q235 carbon steel electrochemical corrosion process; the constant phase element Qf and equivalent resistance Rf denote to the capacitance and resistance of NCDs adsorption film on Q235 carbon steel surface, respectively. Table 4 display the fitting values of EIS parameters (L, Rs, Rf, Qf, n1, Rct, Qdl, n2, χ2 ). The χ2 values in Table 4 were all less than 9.3 × 10−4, which confirmed a satisfactory fit between the fitted and measured values. The Nyquist plots in Fig. 9 elucidate the influence of NCDs concentration on the Q235 carbon steel corrosion process. Table 4 introduces the sum of the sum of Rf and Rct , denoted as Rp , representing the total resistance of the electrochemical reactions during corrosion. A marked increase in Rp value was observed with escalating NCDs concentration. Consequently, the inhibitory effect of the NCDs adsorption film on the Q235 carbon steel substrate arises from both its intrinsic film resistance (Rf ) and its impediment, which amplifies the resistance for the charge transfer process (Rct ) during the corrosion of Q235 carbon steel.

In Table 4, the ηR and θR respectively denote the inhibition efficiencies and surface coverage of the three types of NCDs, as calculated from the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements. These values can be determined using the following equations:

Here, the R
p0 and Rp in Eqs. (10) and (11) are derived from the EIS results, representing the total resistance of corrosion of Q235 carbon steel in the 1 M HCl solution without and with NCDs, respectively. Table 4 reveals a positive correlation between the concentration of NCDs and their inhibitory effect on corrosion. Among the examined NCDs, NCDs-3 demonstrated the most potent inhibition performance, followed sequentially by NCDs-2 and NCDs-1.

The morphological characteristics of Q235 carbon steel after a 24-h immersion in 1 M HCl solution, as well as in the HCl solution containing 200 mg/L of NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, are elucidated in Fig. 11 (a)–(d), respectively. Across these images, Q235 carbon steel consistently exhibits a general corrosion behavior. However, the severity of corrosion varies in the following sequence: Q235HCl > Q235NCDs-1 > Q235NCDs-2 > Q235NCDs-3. These observations showed that among the three types of NCDs, NCDs-3 had best corrosion inhibition property, whereas NCDs-1 had the worst. Furthermore, all three types of NCDs substantially mitigate the corrosion rate of Q235 carbon steel in the HCl solution, without modifying the underlying mechanism or form of the corrosion behavior.

Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations were undertaken to reveal the effect of pyrrolic-N, amino-N, and quaternary-N on the corrosion inhibition property of NCDs. The corrosion inhibition behavior of NCDs is fundamentally tied to their adsorption on the carbon steel surface, a phenomenon intricately linked with electron transfer processes between the carbon steel and NCDs. Drawing on the principles of frontier molecular orbital theory, the electronic properties of NCDs are principally governed by the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). The HOMO characterizes the electron-donating capacity, whereas the LUMO quantifies the electron-accepting potential. In this context, the HOMO/LUMO energy levels of NCDs with various N forms were analyzed to elucidate the manner in which N form modulates the electronic structure and, subsequently, the adsorptive interactions with the carbon steel surface. Figs. 12 , 13 , and 14 exhibit the optimized geometries alongside the computed HOMO/LUMO configurations for NCDs with varying numbers of pyrrolic-N, amino-N, and quaternary-N atoms. In Fig. 12, both HOMO and LUMO of CDs were distributed uniformly. Intriguingly, upon pyrrolic-N doping, a discernible shift in the spatial localization of HOMOs/LUMOs was observed, yet the HOMOs were not exclusively concentrated around the pyrrolic-N sites. Furthermore, an ascending trend in the energy of HOMO (EHOMO) was noted with the increment in number of pyrrolic-N atoms, indicative of the augmentation in the electron-donating prowess of NCDs. All these effects may be due to the p-π conjugation caused by the lone pair electrons of pyrrolic N atoms[54].

The physicochemical parameters of CDs and NCDs with different numbers of pyrrolic-N atoms were computed based on the HOMO and LUMO results, which are listed in Table 5 . The energy gap (E g) in Table 5 is defined as the difference between the EHOMO and the LUMO energy (ELUMO) of CDs or NCDs:

The parameter ΔN , expressing the number of electrons that share at the interface between CDs or NCDs and the Q235 carbon steel, was given by:
(17)

where, χ Fe and χ inh represented the absolute electronegativity of carbon steel and corrosion inhibitors, respectively. Meanwhile, the η Fe and η inh denoted their respective chemical hardness. The value of χ Fe was 4.5 eV, which is the experimental value of work function of the polycrystalline Fe, and the value of η Fe was 0 eV [55].

The value of E g is one of the crucial parameters that can reflect the reactivity of corrosion inhibitors. Those with lower E g values typically exhibit enhanced reactivity, fostering improved interactions with metal surfaces, and thus, with a lower E g value means that the corrosion inhibitor is more likely to be adsorbed on metal surfaces [56]. According to Table 5, reveals that NCDs doped with pyrrolic-N atoms possess lower E g values compared to undoped CDs, suggesting that pyrrolic-N doping with atoms augments the adsorption capacity of NCDs on carbon steel surfaces by increasing their reactivity.

The parameter ΔN is another essential metric for evaluating the adsorptive capabilities of corrosion inhibitors. It was originally proposed to elucidate the electron transfer process between a Lewis base and a Lewis acid, governing the formation of stable bonds. A positive ΔN signifies electron transfer from the Lewis base to the Lewis acid, while a negative value implies the electrons are transferred in the opposite direction [57]. Since metals are considered Lewis acids and corrosion inhibitors as Lewis bases, an inhibitor with a higher positive ΔN may exhibits stronger the electron-donation ability to transfer electrons to the metal surface [26,56]. In Table 4, the ΔN values for CDs and NCDs containing pyrrolic-N atoms are all positive, indicating their potential for adsorption onto metal surfaces through donating electrons to carbon steel. Notably, the ΔN values incremented with the number of pyrrolic-N atoms, exceeding that of pure CDs, indicating that pyrrolic-N doping enhanced NCDs' electron-donating capacity, thereby potentially amplifying their adsorptive performance on carbon steel surfaces.

The calculated HOMOs/LUMOs of the NCDs with different numbers amino-N atoms and quaternary-N atoms are depicted in Figs. 13 and 14, respectively. These illustrations elucidate the discernible alterations in the HOMO/LUMO distribution of NCDs consequent to the doping with amino-N and quaternary-N atoms. Physicochemical parameters listed in Table 4 show that NCDs doped with either amino-N or quaternary-N atoms possessed reduced E g values compared to undoped CDs. Therefore, the doping of amino-N atoms and quaternary-N atoms can enhance the reactivity of NCDs, which may potentially benefit their adsorption on the carbon steel surface. It is worth noting that positive ΔN values, as observed in amino-N-doped CDs, signified electron transfer from NCDs to carbon steel, potentially enhancing their adsorption ability. Conversely, the negative ΔN values found in quaternary-N-doped CDs suggested reverse electron transfer process, potentially facilitating carbon steel dissolution in solutions. This observation leads to an imperative conclusion: doping NCDs with amino-N atoms appeared to augment their adsorptive capabilities on carbon steel surfaces, while quaternary-N atoms might not exert a similar positive effect.

A comparative analysis of the E g and ΔN values for NCDs with pyrrolic-N, amino-N, and quaternary-N further substantiates that pyrrolic-N doping yields the smallest E g and the most positive ΔN. Hence, doping NCDs with pyrrolic-N atoms emerges as a favorable strategy to enhance their adsorption efficiency.

The effect of pyrrolic-N on the adsorption behavior of NCDs was systematically explored using DFT calculations. A comparative analysis was conducted among three forms of N: pyrrolic-N, amino-N, and quaternary-N, each possessing unique electronic properties. Doping pyrrolic-N into NCDs resulted in a reduction in the E g and an increase in the ΔN. This indicates enhanced reactivity and electron-donation ability, leading to a favorable interaction with the carbon steel surface. In the term of amino-N atoms, although they also contributed to a reduction in E g, it was less effective than pyrrolic-N in increasing ΔN, thus displaying moderate influence on the adsorption behavior of NCDs. Surprisingly, NCDs doped with quaternary-N exhibited the lowest E g values. However, their ΔN values were negative, attributed to the positive charge on the quaternary-N atoms [41]. This negative ΔN signified an increase in the electrophilicity of the NCDs, a factor that could potentially accelerate the corrosion process of carbon steel.

The spatial distribution of HOMO/LUMO in NCDs outlines the reactive regions that are favorable for bonding with the carbon steel surface during the adsorption process. Specifically, the HOMO, representing areas within the NCDs that possess the highest energy electrons, plays a pivotal role in electron donation during adsorption. As illustrated in Fig. 12, the HOMO in NCDs containing varying numbers of pyrrolic-N atoms is uniformly distributed. This uniformity arises from the ability of the p-orbital on the nitrogen atom to overlap with the π-orbitals of the pyrrolic ring, forming a p-π conjugated system. In turn, the lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom becomes delocalized over the entire ring, contributing to the evenly distributed nature of the HOMO. This uniform distribution ensures that the reactive sites are consistently dispersed across the NCDs, enabling parallel adsorption on the carbon steel surface through the donation of π-electrons to the Fe atoms. The doping of pyrrolic-N, therefore, appears to enhance the interaction between NCDs and the carbon steel surface, potentially amplifying the corrosion inhibition efficacy of the NCDs.

The mechanism of NCDs inhibiting the corrosion of carbon steel in HCl solution is depicted in Fig. 15 , and can be summarized as follows. Carbon steel experiences hydrogen evolution corrosion in this acidic milieu, driven by the anodic dissolution of Fe atoms and the cathodic reduction of H+ ions. Influenced by polar water molecules, Fe atoms are converted into Fe2+ ions and subsequently leave the lattice, dissolving in the solution. Electrons remaining in the lattice are captured by H+ ions adsorbed on the carbon steel surface, contributing to the hydrogen reduction process. The continuous cathodic and anodic reactions lead to rapid hydrogen evolution corrosion of carbon steel. Upon introduction of NCDs into the HCl solution, they migrate to the carbon steel surface via thermal motion and adsorb in parallel, donating their π-electrons to the Fe atoms at the interface. Once a sufficient amount of NCDs are adsorbed, they form a protective film on the carbon steel surface. This film functions as an effective barrier at the interface between the carbon steel and the HCl solution, inhibiting both the dissolution of iron atoms and the reduction of H+ ions by preventing the direct contact between carbon steel and both water molecules and H+ ions. The inclusion of pyrrolic-N atoms within the NCDs significantly enhances their adsorption capability by modulating the π-electron energy. This modulation is facilitated through the delocalization of the lone pair of electrons on the pyrrolic-N atom into the π-conjugated system, thereby bolstering both the reactivity and electron-donation ability of the NCDs. In contrast, quaternary-N atoms impede the electron-donation ability of NCDs, attributable to their positive charge. Amino-N atoms, on the other hand, are ineffective in augmenting the reactivity of NCDs, since their lone pair electrons in the p-orbital can’ t form p-π conjugation with the six-member rings in NCDs.

In this study, NCDs were synthesized using a two-step hydrothermal synthesis method. In the initial step, CDs were formed by heating a citric acid solution at 200 °C for 5 h. Subsequently, NCDs were prepared by heating a solution containing ammonia solution and the CDs obtained in the first step for 5 h. Three heating temperatures were employed during this second step: 100 °C, 160 °C, and 220 °C, resulting in the corresponding synthesized NCDs labeled as NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3, respectively. The structural characteristics and corrosion inhibition properties of these NCDs in 1 M HCl solution were extensively evaluated, leading to the following key findings:
(1)
The XPS results showed that, as the synthesis temperature increased, the nitrogen content of NCDs exhibited an initial increase followed by a decrease, but notably, the content of pyrrolic-N consistently increased. The contents of pyrrolic-N of three types of NCDs followed the order that: NCDs-3(4.10 at.%) > NCDs-2(3.18 at.%) > NCDs-1(2.30 at.%), respectively.
(2)
The corrosion inhibition efficiency of NCDs-1, NCDs-2, and NCDs-3 at a concentration of 200 mg/L could reached 88.40 %, 92.56 %, and 96.63 %, respectively. The order of inhibition efficiency of the three inhibitors, from highest to lowest, was consistent with the order of their pyrrolic-N content. This observation highlighted the significance of pyrrolic-N doping in enhancing the corrosion inhibition properties of NCDs.
(3)
Quantum chemical calculations shed light on the inhibition mechanism of NCDs, revealing that they adsorb parallelly onto the carbon steel surface by donating π-electrons to the Fe atoms. This interaction leads to the formation of a protective film that isolates the carbon steel surface from the HCl solution, effectively inhibiting the dissolution of Fe atoms and the reduction of H+ ions. A comparative analysis of pyrrolic-N with quaternary-N and amino-N demonstrated that pyrrolic-N was the most efficacious in augmenting the adsorption ability of NCDs, thereby enhancing their reactivity and electron-donation capability.
